Name: Josh Allen
Outlook: He is him. While technically Allen finished with his lowest fantasy points per game average since 2019, he continued his streak of being the QB1 or QB2 for the sixth consecutive season. In 2024, Allen became the first top-5 fantasy QB without a top-36 scoring WR or top-12 TE. In 2025, he did it again. Same exact thing. Yes, he is now 30 years old, and the Bills mixed things up, firing HC Sean McDermott. The offense should remain the same, with the WR corps hopefully upgraded with the addition of DJ Moore. Allen is our unquestioned QB1 and the first pick in 2QB/SuperFlex leagues. However, in 1QB leagues, we caution taking him at the end of Round 2 and would prefer to load up on RBs or WRs.
Dynasty: Allen is the all-time leader in fantasy points per game and career rushing TDs (79) for a QB… and we are only entering Year 9 in the NFL. Bask in his greatness and remind your league mates that Allen is him.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Lamar Jackson
Outlook: For the first three weeks of the season, it was vintage Lamar, averaging 27.6 fantasy points on the back of three passing TDs and 39 rushing yards per game. Unfortunately, a back injury altered his season and the future of the Baltimore Ravens. After returning in Week 9, he never quite looked like himself, averaging just 13.5 fantasy points, 1.2 passing TDs, and 20 rushing yards per game. With John Harbaugh out, it feels like a different era in Baltimore. Lamar’s raw pass attempts remain low yet continue to be the most valuable in fantasy: 40% were 10+ air yards (second highest in NFL) and 9% were “end-zone” attempts, the highest rate in the NFL. He remains in the annual top-5 QB conversation, but we would prefer if he slipped into the fourth or fifth round in redraft.
Dynasty: At 29 years old, we still are in prime territory for a run of multiple dominant fantasy seasons. He is our consensus QB5 in startups. Don’t be the idiot sandwich in your league who got too cute trading away Lamar because you thought the “value” was too good to pass up.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jayden Daniels
Outlook: After a storybook rookie season, the Washington Commanders nosedived in Year 2 of the Jayden Daniels era as he struggled with injuries (knee, hamstring, and elbow) before being shut down in Week 15 for the season. His rushing numbers remained on par with 2024, but the cast of receivers certainly let him down as 11% of his passes were dropped, the highest rate in the NFL. Daniels can be a difference-making fantasy asset due to his legs, but drafters are not giving any sort of discount this year. In other words, you will have to pay up for what he did as a rookie and the hope that he can return to top-5 status.
Dynasty: Jayden won Offensive Rookie of the Year in 2024 and took the league by storm. Then in 2025, he recorded just ONE top-12 fantasy finish. We still view him as a top-tier fantasy QB given his elite rushing ability, but his true ceiling is at least worth questioning in the short term, given the lack of continuity at OC and barren skill group.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Drake Maye
Outlook: The signs were there in Year 1 that Maye was likely going to be a good starter in this league, but what he did in Year 2 was amazing. He was barely edged out by Matthew Stafford for league MVP, but in any other season, Maye easily could have taken home that award. He led the league in EPA per play and completion percentage while finishing inside the top-five at the position in fantasy points per drop back, which is pretty insane considering his porous offensive line and a middling pass-catching group. Add A.J. Brown to the mix and we are still bullish on the young superstar. Through 28 full games in his career, he is averaging 31 rushing yards per game, which gives him a weekly floor to finish as a top-5 QB again.
Dynasty: Still only 23 years old, Maye is now firmly locked in as a top-tier dynasty QB for years to come, thanks to his fantasy-friendly skill set. He sports the fourth-highest pass success rate of any QB through their 1st 2 years in the NFL behind only Mahomes, Purdy, and Marino.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jalen Hurts
Outlook: Are fantasy managers experiencing name fatigue? Hurts is coming off a season with the worst consistency metrics of his career, including averaging just 16.3 fantasy points per game from Week 9 on. We saw his fewest TDs inside the 5-yard line and now with AJ Brown leaving and yet another OC change, perhaps fantasy managers are scared off. While Hurts isn't a slouch as a passer, we love him in fantasy for his ability to use his legs. The overall Philly offense should return to form, making Hurts a relative value compared to where he was being drafted in the past.
Dynasty: We are not trying to bury the recent Super Bowl MVP, but if someone in your league still views him as a locked-in top-5 QB, we are willing to listen to offers. We still view Hurts as a top-10 dynasty QB, but it is fair to wonder where this thing goes over the next 2-3 years if the Tush Push gets banned.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Joe Burrow
Outlook: Through six seasons as Cincy’s signal-caller, Burrow has three elite fantasy seasons and three injury-plagued disappointments. In 2025, he went down in Week 2 and did not return until Week 13. Those final six weeks were vintage Burrow as he put together a 17-game pace of 4500+ passing yards and 43 passing TDs. It also helps that the Cincy defense has a ton of holes, forcing shootouts and more passing volume Burrow’s way. While we don’t love living and dying with TD rates from pocket passers, Burrow runs just enough to ignore that rule. They throw enough inside the red zone to justify his ADP.
Dynasty: With Ja’Marr Chase and Tee Higgins locked up for at least the next three years, you have to be cackling to yourself if you are a Burrow manager. He is a weekly threat to finish as QB1, and at only 29 years old, he feels so safe as a top-5 dynasty QB in startups. Now if only the Bengals could help improve his .389 winning percentage in September…
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Trevor Lawrence
Outlook: 2025 was by far Lawrence’s best fantasy campaign thanks to whatever pep talk the team received during their Week 8 bye. From then on, he was the QB1 for fantasy, averaging 22.6 fantasy points per game. He joined Josh Allen as the only QBs in NFL history with 4,000+ passing yards, 29+ passing TDs, and 9+ rushing TDs in the same season. Credit goes to Liam Coen, who transformed this offense in the second half despite a WR room that dropped 8.6% of TLaw’s pass attempts, tied for the highest rate of any QB over the last four years. Lawrence is not being overdrafted based on last year’s finish, so we are still in for 1QB leagues if he is available near the double-digit rounds.
Dynasty: It was quite the boom year for Lawrence as his rushing production spiked with his most attempts, yards, and carries inside the 5 of his career. Only 26 years old, expect the rushing TDs to regress, but the Jags look like a legit contender in the AFC. We view Lawrence as a back-end QB1 but would prefer him as a high-end QB2 in SuperFlex leagues.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jaxson Dart
Outlook: After the Giants traded up in the first round to take Dart, he exceeded expectations as a rookie, averaging 41 rushing yards per game in his starts, the fifth best among rookie QBs ever. He tied Drake Maye for fourth-best in fantasy points per dropback last year despite attempting three total passes to Malik Nabers before he went down. Dart’s upside is enticing despite the fact that he plays with reckless abandon. His ADP is not accounting for a Year 2 leap, making him a prime candidate to roll with as a late-round QB.
Dynasty: There are some variables at play with a new coaching staff, offensive play-caller, and the recovery timeline for Malik Nabers. Nevertheless, Dart is a mobile QB on a young, ascending offense that seems to provide fantasy-friendly results. His playstyle might invite more risk, but in dynasty, we view him as a low-end QB1.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Dak Prescott
Outlook: Another year and another opportunity for fantasy managers to disrespect the Dallas franchise QB. His ten top-10 weekly finishes tied Josh Allen and Matthew Stafford for most among QBs as he led the NFL in completions and set career highs in passing yards and attempts. He did have massive splits in Cowboys wins (24.0 ppg) and losses (12.7), the largest gap of any QB in 2025. Blessed with a historically bad defense, Cowboys games averaged 58 combined points, always giving Dak and company a chance to jump into a shootout.  We love us some Dak.
Dynasty: His age (almost 33) keeps his dynasty value at bay, but he is under contract for another three years. In SuperFlex leagues, he is the type of QB2 we love trying to work a deal for during the lulls of the off-season.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Caleb Williams
Outlook: Williams set the Bears’ all-time passing yardage mark (3,942), improved his pressure-to-sack ratio mightily, and had five games of 24+ fantasy points in Year 2. However, the erratic play still frustrates fantasy managers as he completed just 58% of his passes, a mark surpassing only Shedeur Sanders and J.J. McCarthy. He was more effective as a runner, including adding three scores on the ground. In Year 2 of the Ben Johnson system, perhaps there is room for Caleb to fly even higher and finish as a top-5 QB.
Dynasty: We are lower than the market on Caleb, putting him inside our top-10 QBs, but firmly two tiers behind the other top-end guys. The upside is tantalizing, especially entering his age-24 season with one of the best young pass-catching corps in the league. He is a hold, considering anyone trading away Caleb wants an absolute haul.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jared Goff
Outlook: Impress your friends by letting them know the only QBs to finish inside the top-10 at the position each of the last four years: Josh Allen, Jalen Hurts, and… drumroll… Jared Goff. The first half was middling with just one top-12 finish, but he responded when things mattered for fantasy, averaging 20.3 fantasy points per game from Weeks 10-16. He imploded in Week 17 at Minnesota (two INTs, three fumbles, five sacks), but that is the game you play with Goff. He offers nothing in the run game, keeping his ceiling around that QB10 mark. The Lions are favored in 14 of their first 15 games, including a juicy Week 1 matchup at home against the Saints. We know what Goff is at this point: a streaky streamer who remains one of the best at dicing up zone coverages.
Dynasty: Goff is the perfect QB2 in dynasty attached to a high-powered offense, yet likely undervalued by your league mates. He is signed through 2028 and somehow still only 31 years old.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Brock Purdy
Outlook: After missing half the season with a toe injury, fantasy managers hopped on Purdy’s back in the fantasy playoffs as he finished the season as the QB5, 4, and 1. Always underappreciated in fantasy, his 6.2% career TD rate is the fourth best ever for a QB through their first 50 games. We always want to buy into the 49ers offense as long as CMC is alive and well; with the addition of Mike Evans, perhaps Purdy can find the formula to enter into top-10 territory at the position.
Dynasty: Purdy has been one of the most efficient starting QBs in the NFL since he took over for San Fran a few years ago. Kyle Shanahan's scheme is just too good, and Purdy is a great fit for it. He signed a big-time extension last off-season, so he is locked up for the next several years.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Justin Herbert
Outlook: It is a miracle Big Herbs is still alive. After losing both starting All-Pro tackles, he took a career-high number of hits and was sacked 54 times, easily the most of his career. Even with all that, he managed to bank a QB9 finish, thanks mostly to career highs in rush attempts (83) and rushing yards (498). If that part of his game can stick around and his line can stay healthy, the weapons are there for Herbert to have one of the best seasons of his career in 2026. Mike McDaniel is one of the most creative play-callers in the league, so we are bullish on Herbert if you can get him late in your drafts.
Dynasty: Herbert became the first QB since 2018 (Deshaun Watson) to lead the league in % of drop backs pressured (43%) and make the NFL playoffs. That type of NFL life isn’t sustainable for the 28-year-old QB, but we expect 2026 to be much better. Big Herbs is a top-10 dynasty QB in our book.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Bo Nix
Outlook: Nix disappointed in a sophomore season where he led the NFL in pass attempts and the Broncos were the AFC's No. 1 seed. Apart from three monster games, he was relatively uninspiring as a fantasy starter, including six weeks scoring below 15 fantasy points. Despite having one of the best offensive lines in the business, he tied Shedeur Sanders and J.J. McCarthy for lowest yards per pass attempt when pressured. We still are in on Nix for fantasy with the arrival of Jaylen Waddle and an off season of recovering from his brutal ankle injury. Consider pairing him with another late-round QB as you see how he responds as a runner in the first month of the season.
Dynasty: We have Nix ranked outside the top-12 of dynasty QBs, but others in your league might still think he is a top-10 guy. Only 26 years old, he is a hold but will likely settle into the low-end QB1/high-end QB2 guy, a far cry from the hoopla after last off-season.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Baker Mayfield
Outlook: In Weeks 1-8, the magic we saw from “Laser” Mayfield in 2024 looked like it was here to stay. He was in the MVP conversation on the back of a 13:2 TD-to-INT ratio with the team eyeing the postseason. However, after the bye, Baker stumbled, throwing nine INTs while struggling to get there for fantasy. He battled through some injuries, his OL was banged up, and his pass-catchers never quite felt in sync. With Mike Evans gone, there are some question marks with the offense that now employs its fifth different play-caller in five years. Baker is another late-round QB that should only be drafted in 1QB leagues if you buy into Tampa Bay’s early season schedule.
Dynasty: We are still believers in Mayfield, but Tampa does have some key decisions on their hands as he is in the final year of his current deal and entering his age-31 season. His 7.2% TD rate in 2024 looks a bit fluky, so instead of having a locked-in QB1, we have him more as a mid-tier QB2.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Patrick Mahomes
Outlook: After a year in which the Chiefs failed to make the playoffs, and Mahomes tore his left ACL in Week 15, we have to reassess the aura surrounding the Kansas City QB. It is going to be really fascinating to track his recovery all offseason as he comes off the ACL plus LCL surgery. For fantasy, it is highly unlikely we will see the rushing production we saw in 2025, when Mahomes set career highs in per-game rushing attempts, yards, and TDs. The Chiefs are gifted with a “third-place” schedule and a core group willing to go for one more title run. The addition of Kenneth Walker III could take some pressure off Mahomes trying to Superman this thing every week. For fantasy purposes, he is a low-end QB1 with a name carrying more weight in your home leagues than actual fantasy-winning production.
Dynasty: Mahomes is the dude in KC. Forever and always...he is under contract through 2031. However, the star has lost some of his shine as we would consider him a solid but unspectacular top-10 QB in dynasty. Listen, we would love to have him on our rosters, but he is certainly not an “untouchable” in SuperFlex leagues.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Kyler Murray
Outlook: The Kyler Murray era in Arizona officially came to a close with the Cardinals releasing the former No. 1 overall pick in early March. Still just 28 years old, Kyler will look to get his career back on track in Minnesota, perhaps following the path of guys like Kirk Cousins and Sam Darnold. Expect the team to frame this as a QB competition throughout the summer, but if you watched JJ "Nine" McCarthy play NFL football in 2025, you likely know where this is headed in terms of the Week 1 starter. We are still keeping the light on for Kyler to return to being a top-10 fantasy QB.
Dynasty: This move makes all the sense in the world for Kyler and Kevin O'Connell's team. They are short on cash this year in terms of cap space, and with Arizona picking up the vast majority of his salary this year, they were able to sign him for the vet minimum. Kyler is looking to parlay a year of starting in Minnesota into another long-term starting gig, ala Sam Darnold. He is a high upside swing in SuperFlex if you think his rushing upside and change of scenery can restore him to QB1 status.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Matthew Stafford
Outlook: It was an all-time season for the future Hall of Famer, becoming one of six QBs ever with 4700 passing yards & 46 TDs in a season. It wasn’t just stat-padding, as his nine top-6 finishes were the most among all QBs. 33 of his 46 TDs were inside the red zone, by far the most in the league. We caution going all-in on non-mobile QBs coming off a year where his TD rate went to the moon. Stafford will likely settle into low-end QB1 territory, but you have an opportunity to scoop him up as your team’s QB2 to pair with another.
Dynasty: Is this a chance for one last ride? Every year, we ask that question, knowing the 38-year-old has one final year under contract with the Rams. With $408 million in career earnings, Stafford has made more money than any other player in NFL history. It is probably best just to ride off into the dynasty sunset with him.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Tyler Shough
Outlook: Tyler Shough truthers, take a bow. Entering the 2025 NFL Draft cycle, a lot was made about Shough's age, but credit to New Orleans, they seem to have found a diamond in the rough. Over his final five starts, he averaged 20 fantasy points per game, a 70% completion percentage, and 27 rushing yards per game. He unlocked Chris Olave and showed out with the best completion percentage in the intermediate area of the field (11-19 yards). Believe in Kellen Moore as a play-caller and the addition of eighth overall pick Jordyn Tyson to the WR room. Shough is one of our favorite late-round targets in drafts.
Dynasty: Shough defied the ageists and became a legit fantasy threat down the stretch. Nevertheless, it seems many are pausing, calling the season a fluke. As a QB2 in SuperFlex, he could be acquired potentially with a late first-round rookie pick.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Malik Willis
Outlook: Willis' time in Tennessee was pretty forgettable, to say the least. The team took him in the third round back in 2022 and moved on after just three starts in two years. However, he landed in Green Bay as Jordan Love's backup and now has a chance to revitalize his career as a starter after getting some great coaching from Matt LaFleur. We have seen other young QBs (Baker Mayfield, Sam Darnold, Daniel Jones, for example) take similar paths: dumped by their original organization, sit and learn for a couple of years as a backup, and then get a second chance at a starting gig elsewhere. Could Willis be next? For fantasy, the Dolphins project to be among the worst teams in the league with straight buns at WR, but we know his mobility (41, 73, & 60 rushing yards in three starts last year) works at the QB position for the game we play. Willis certainly has the wheels to put up fantasy-friendly numbers and is worth monitoring early on in the year as a streamer.
Dynasty: The red flags are there: Willis has never attempted more than 23 passes in a game in his six career starts, and the Dolphins could be terrible in 2026. The window to sell Willis is likely gone after Jaylen Waddle was dealt. Expecting anything more than one full year starting in the NFL might be wishful thinking.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jordan Love
Outlook: Love's 23 passing TDs and 3300+ yards might not stand out, but efficiency has always been the name of his game. He ranked sixth in Passer Rating, seventh in completion percentage over expectation, and tied Patrick Mahomes for 12th in fantasy points per dropback.  He remains one of the more polarizing starters in the league, as we have seen some really high-end play at times, yet he hasn't put it all together for a full season. For fantasy purposes, we are viewing Love as a streamer to start the season as Green Bay begins the year favored in each of their first six matchups.
Dynasty: Only 27 years old, Love is the type of QB in dynasty who likely costs 85 cents on the dollar compared to "brand name QBs". Over his last three seasons as a starter, he has a higher TD rate (5.5%) and Passer Rating (97.8) than Josh Allen and TD passes (80) than Patrick Mahomes. He remains a rock-solid QB to have on your dynasty roster, and he has proven he's the guy for LaFleur's team. Love signed a four-year extension recently, so he is under contract through 2028.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Cam Ward
Outlook: The Titans took Cam Ward first overall in the 2025 NFL Draft, and this is the perfect example of what can happen to a young, talented QB in their first year in the league. No disrespect to the good folks of Nashville, but this was set up to fail from the get-go as the Titans trailed on 72% of his dropbacks, fifth highest in the NFL. He tied Geno Smith for the most sacks taken (55!) and finished with just one top-12 finish on the year, albeit against Super Bowl Champion Seattle in Week 12. The addition of Wan’Dale Robinson and fourth overall pick Carnell Tate gives him a shot at a Year 2 breakout. However, unless he starts running more, Ward will likely be left amongst a pile of other QBs you could stream, but need a big spike in TD rate to matter.
Dynasty: His 5.9 yards per attempt ranks 32nd among 35 first-round QBs over the last decade. Woof. For fantasy, he ranked dead last among all QBs in fantasy points per dropback - Ward is not running enough to overcome the offense’s shortcomings. However, in dynasty leagues, he will carry QB2 value as long as he has the starting job.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: C.J. Stroud
Outlook: The decline is painful to watch as Stroud went from fantasy darling as a rookie in 2023 to an afterthought and liability the last two seasons. He has averaged just 218 passing yards per game with six total top-12 finishes, the same number he totaled as a rookie. With arguably the best defense in the NFL, the Texans do not need Stroud to put up gaudy numbers to compete. He is stuck in redraft purgatory as a QB likely sitting on your waiver wire to be picked up only for plus matchups.
Dynasty: We loved Stroud as a player coming out of Ohio State and felt justified with an awesome rookie campaign. However, he is relegated to back-end QB2 territory despite his youth. The Texans’ defense and his lack of elite rushing numbers will keep his ceiling capped. The Texans extended his fifth-year option, so expect a long-term contract to be worked out in the near future.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Sam Darnold
Outlook: Oh, the tales we get to tell our grandchildren about Sam Darnold resurrecting from the fantasy grave! The Super Bowl Champion has 60(!) passing TDs over the last two seasons (tied for fifth most in the NFL) with the second-best yards per pass attempt (8.2) in that span. His passing volume declined down the stretch as Seattle came up with a winning formula, leaning on the run and a play-action-based offense that hyper-targeted Jaxon Smith-Njigba. We are happy for the man Darnold, but he still belongs only in the streaming fantasy category despite his success.
Dynasty: Hindsight is always 20/20, but hat tip to this front office and coaching staff for that Geno deal (sorry, Raider fans). Sam Darnold signed a three-year deal with $55 (!!) million guaranteed last offseason, so we know he is the dude for the foreseeable future after finally getting the monkey off his back and taking his team to the Super Bowl. For fantasy, Darnold profiles as more of a dynasty QB2 as he only averaged about 14 fantasy PPG, but this recent success solidifies him as a locked-in starter...not something we could say as early as two years ago!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Daniel Jones
Outlook: For the first eight weeks of the season, Jones was the QB6, averaging 20.5 fantasy points per game and a scintillating 71% completion percentage. The Colts were 8-2 heading into their Week 11 bye, and then the world came crashing down with a brutal season-ending Achilles tear. Our injury timeline suggests he will be limited throughout the off-season and could be compromised early in the 2026 season, especially as a runner.
Dynasty: The Colts brought back the 30-year-old Jones on a two-year deal worth up to $100 million. The front office and coaching staff are essentially betting their jobs on Jones returning to form. We would make the opposite bet for fantasy, willing to sell last season’s high if someone in your league thinks they are finding a solution at QB for SuperFlex leagues.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jacoby Brissett
Outlook: Where’s the Beef? Brissett was cookin’ last year for fantasy purposes, averaging over 40 pass attempts per game in his starts. His nine consecutive top-12 fantasy finishes were the most of 2025, thanks in large part to the team trailing on 73% of his dropbacks. Despite the fact that the Cardinals went 1-11 in his starts, he remains the incumbent veteran starter with Kyler Murray now donning Viking purple. He remains on the streaming radar, although we expect the passing volume to slightly decrease with new head coach Mike LaFleur calling the shots.
Dynasty: You need to be counting your blessings in a SuperFlex dynasty league that the 33-year-old Brissett continues to be relevant as a QB2. The window of his opportunity will be closing as the Cardinals look forward to finding a new franchise QB.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Bryce Young
Outlook: First, the good: Young led his Panthers to an NFC South title and a close loss to the Rams in the Wild Card Round of the Playoffs. He also set career-highs in completion percentage (64%), passing TDs (23), and rush attempts. Yet, he exceeded 20+ fantasy points in just three games on the season and felt impossible to get a read on week-to-week. The NFC South is a weak division, but unless we see a spike in his scramble rate and pass attempts, topping 25 passing TDs would be a small miracle. It feels tough finding routes for Young to break through as anything more than a mid-QB2 for fantasy.
Dynasty: The Panthers picked up Young’s fifth-year option, but there is still debate about whether he is the organization’s long-term answer at QB. In 44 career starts, he has been a top-12 QB in just 23% of them. He still has a lot of life left in the NFL, but unless we get another upgrade to the pass-catchers, it will be hard seeing him will his way to anything more than a mid-to-low end QB2 in dynasty. Young seems like a prime candidate to trade away in SuperFlex leagues if he is a luxury QB3 for your roster and someone else is desperate for the position.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Aaron Rodgers
Outlook: Rodgers spent the majority of last offseason pondering his NFL future before landing in Pittsburgh on a one-year deal with Mike Tomlin. In classic Steelers fashion, Rodgers did enough to help them stay competitive and make the playoffs, but they ultimately fell short in the Wild Card Round. Back with the Steelers again, at this point in his career, the vet isn't really on the radar for fantasy outside of 2QB formats.
Dynasty: Now 42 years old, Rodgers and the Steelers seemed like a one-year fling, but they hired his old coach, Mike McCarthy, to replace Tomlin. In dynasty, you are constantly guessing on the next plot twist in the reality show known as Aaron Rodgers.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Geno Smith
Outlook: You cannot make this stuff up - the Jets traded for Geno Smith, coming full circle on a player they once took in the second round of the NFL Draft way back in 2013! He was a shell of himself last year in Las Vegas, ranking dead last among all QBs in sack rate (10.6%) and INTs (17). For fantasy purposes, Geno is off the radar, but we would be satisfied if he gives Garrett Wilson 200 targets. At some point, expect the Jets to eventually replace him with fourth-round rookie Cade Klubnik for a few starts as they look to 2027’s QB class.
Dynasty: Consider him a bridge-type QB as they likely attack the position aggressively in the 2027 NFL Draft. It is wild to think Geno has been around since 2013.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Shedeur Sanders
Outlook: Sanders wasn’t that bad, right? Welp, after checking the numbers, we can report back that it was hot garbage ranking dead last in catchable target rate (66.5%), completion percentage (56.6%), and Passer Rating (68.1). He did have one week for fantasy (QB2 vs. TEN in Week 14) that gave you a glimpse of the upside, but there is no guarantee for him to be the long-term solution for Cleveland. We are monitoring the off-season news and new HC Todd Monken’s words regarding the competition between Sanders and Deshaun Watson. Even in SuperFlex leagues, we shy away from expecting anything out of the former fifth-round pick.
Dynasty: Among 40 rookie QBs with 7+ starts over the last decade, Sanders ranked 38th in pass success rate and 39th in INT rate. It was barf-worthy, and a year from now, he could easily be an irrelevant name in SuperFlex leagues. Shedeur truthers like Mel Kiper Jr. must be in shambles.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Michael Penix Jr.
Outlook: The Falcons were roasted for surprisingly taking Penix eighth overall in 2024, and the results thus far are barf-worthy. In two seasons, Penix has started 12 total games while completing less than 60% of his passes. For context, that ranks 29th among 33 QBs taken in the first round of NFL Drafts over the last decade. He suffered a torn ACL in Week 11 last year, and off-season reports suggest the team is not rushing his rehab with Tua Tagovailoa now in tow as competition.
Dynasty: It feels like a make-or-break season for Penix entering Year 3 under a new regime with a new head coach, new GM, and new president (former QB Matt Ryan). Averaging under seven rushing yards per game in his 12 career starts, it is hard to paint a picture where Penix becomes anything more in fantasy than a bar conversation with your buddies: “Hey, remember when Atlanta paid Kirk Cousins AND drafted Michael Penix Jr.?!?!”
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Fernando Mendoza
Outlook: Mendoza’s storybook college career is one of the most compelling parts of the No. 1 overall pick. From his humble beginnings as a lightly recruited three-star QB to transferring to Indiana and having a season for the ages, which ended in an undefeated national championship. He jumped in every single meaningful statistical category and became Indiana’s first-ever Heisman Trophy winner and the first in the Big Ten since 2006. The Raiders are starting fresh with play-caller Klint Kubiak at the helm. In Year 1, Mendoza will likely take some lumps. He is off the redraft radar, waiting his turn to start behind veteran Kirk Cousins.
Dynasty: When you mix in a relatively weak draft class with Mendoza’s winning mentality, production, and precision passing, it is not hard to see why the Raiders would envision him as their heir apparent at QB. In SuperFlex, we have him in the 1.05 range behind the elite WRs. Depending on your strategy and team needs, Mendoza is not a must-target, but we can see the path of him eventually being a low-end QB1 for dynasty.

Name: Kirk Cousins
Outlook: After two middling years in Atlanta, Cousins signed a “five-year” contract with Las Vegas, but only $11.3 million was guaranteed. He reunites with Klint Kubiak, who was his play-caller during his time in Minnesota. Expect Captain Kirk to potentially start the beginning of the season before giving way to No. 1 overall pick Fernando Mendoza. In other words, Cousins is a first-round pick in your “QB-mentors-only” leagues but off the radar for fantasy in any other format.
Dynasty: Uncle Kirk is loaded! With $321 million in career earnings, Cousins trails only Tom Brady and Matthew Stafford in his quest to somehow earn more money than any other player in NFL history. In dynasty, he has a limited opportunity to start at the beginning of the year before Mendoza takes over.

Name: Tua Tagovailoa
Outlook: Tua’s time in Miami came to a crashing end as he was cut for an NFL-record $99.2 million in dead cap money. The Falcons, scooping up every known left-handed QB in the universe, signed him for the veteran minimum ($1 million) to pair with Michael Penix Jr., who is returning from a season-ending ACL injury. Tua’s yards per attempt declined in three straight seasons (8.9 > 8.3 > 7.2 > 6.9), signaling that the former fifth overall pick’s best days are likely behind him. Nevertheless, he could begin the season as Atlanta’s starter, but is irrelevant to the fantasy conversation.
Dynasty: After signing a four-year $212 million deal in July of 2024, Tua went 12-13 and finished as a top-12 QB in just a quarter of those starts. The 28-year-old is a QB3 at best in SuperFlex but likely will be supplanted by Penix at some point in 2026.

Name: Carson Beck
Outlook: The Cardinals selected Beck in the third round, making him the third QB taken in the draft. His experience (43 college starts) certainly helped his cause after the team brought him in for a top-30 visit. He can get the ball out quickly on 3-step drops and hit his receivers in stride in zone coverage. His arm strength is subpar, but it should not shock anyone if Beck ends up finishing the season as the Cardinals' starting QB on a team destined for a top-5 pick.
Dynasty: It feels like Beck has been part of the college football conversation for a while as the backup on Georgia’s two-time National Championship teams before being the starter for 2023 & 2024. The weapons surrounding him are certainly intriguing, so take a swing at Beck in the third round of your rookie drafts. He might quickly return to irrelevance if Arizona takes a QB in 2027.

Name: Deshaun Watson
Outlook: This is a tricky situation in Cleveland. We have not seen Watson be meaningful in fantasy since (checks… rechecks) 2020! Seriously, he averaged 13.2 fantasy points per game in 18 starts over multiple years, dealing with injuries and the scrutiny of NFL fans and the media. He will battle Shedeur Sanders for first-team reps this summer, but is nowhere near consideration for fantasy.
Dynasty: The Browns converted nearly $45 million of his salary into a signing bonus for the third year in a row, kicking the can down the road for arguably the worst sports contract of all-time. He is in the final year of his deal, but in dynasty, he is basically worthless as a potential short-term QB3.
